title: Module Check: Backtracking, Union-Find, and Remaining Interview Patterns

questions.question: In backtracking, why must you `result.append(path[:])` instead of `result.append(path)`?
questions.answer: path is a mutable list; appending the reference stores a pointer that gets modified (and eventually emptied) by later exploration. `path[:]` makes a shallow copy — a snapshot of the current state — that remains unchanged as recursion continues.

questions.question: Name the two optimisations that give Union-Find O(α(n)) amortised complexity and briefly describe each.
questions.answer: Path compression flattens the parent chain during find by repointing nodes toward the root. Union by rank attaches the smaller-rank tree under the larger-rank root so the structure does not grow tall unnecessarily. Together they make repeated connectivity operations effectively constant for interview-scale input sizes.

questions.question: Why does this course usually place DP after the highest-frequency patterns for general prep, and when would you move it earlier?
questions.answer: Because the strongest interview-prep sequences prioritise the higher-frequency patterns first for short sprints, while DP is more costly to learn. You would move DP earlier if your target company or interview track is more DP-heavy, such as Google-style or more advanced algorithmic interviews.

questions.question: Which linked-list technique is the best default when a problem may modify the true head node?
questions.explanation: A dummy or sentinel node removes head-specific branching and makes pointer rewiring uniform. The other options are unrelated topics.

questions.question: What is the most accurate description of this course's trie coverage?

options.A: Trie is fully taught as a first-wave core pattern that should be studied before trees
options.B: Trie is taught as a source-backed second-wave pattern with O(m) operations and prefix recognition cues
options.C: Trie is excluded from interview prep because it never appears in common problem sets
options.D: Trie belongs only to competitive programming, not technical interviews

questions.explanation: The lesson now teaches the trie template directly, but the study-sequence evidence still places tries behind the first-wave array/tree/graph patterns rather than ahead of them.
